nativeRange.habitat: The Fiddler Stingray is found along the eastern coast of Australia, from approximately the Queensland–New South Wales border south to Victoria — a stretch of coastline running from roughly 28°S to 38°S. It inhabits sandy and seagrass-covered seafloor, often near rocky reef edges, and ranges from the shallows of the intertidal zone down to about 100 m depth.

decorPreferences: Deep, soft sand substrate (4+ inches) to allow burying
Large open sandy footprint (front-to-back depth of at least 90 cm advised)
Smooth rockwork along tank perimeter only — avoid sharp or abrasive decor
Rounded tank corners preferred to reduce collision injuries
Minimal vertical structure; species occupies the bottom almost exclusively

diet.notes: In the wild, feeds mainly on crabs and shrimps and acts as an active benthic scavenger. In captivity, has been reported to accept cleaned squid, live or frozen ghost shrimp, fresh marine fish pieces, and scallops. New arrivals frequently refuse food initially; live prey is commonly used to stimulate the first feeding response.

goodWith: Large peaceful fish that occupy the upper water column
Tankmates too large to be consumed and not prone to fin-nipping
avoidWith: Crustaceans and small molluscs (will be hunted as prey)
Small fish that may fit in the ray's mouth
Other rays (territorial stress and injury risk in shared substrate)

imageCandidates.notes: Photo by Sylke Rohrlach (Jervis Bay, NSW), 4000x3000. Whole animal in sharp side profile over seagrass, showing the complete banjo/guitarfish body shape (rounded disc, long shark-like tail, dorsal fins) and the dark-edged banding on the sandy-brown disc; best overall representation.
Photo by Taso Viglas, 3648x2736. Sharp close-up of the disc showing the bold dark-edged maze/band pattern in vivid detail; tail partly out of frame so kept as a non-recommended detail shot.
Photo by Sylke Rohrlach, 2347x3129. Front-on view showing the diagnostic dark-edged banding and the eyes; different angle for variety, slight kelp intrusion at top edge.

summary: The Fiddler Stingray (Trygonorrhina fasciata), also known as the Eastern Fiddler Ray or Banjo Ray, is a subtropical Australian elasmobranch with bold dark-edged banding on a sandy-brown disc. It is generally regarded as an expert-only species due to its large adult size and demanding husbandry.
careNotes:
The Fiddler Stingray is a subtropical, bottom-dwelling ray endemic to eastern Australian coastal waters and is generally considered suitable only for very large, public-aquarium-style displays. Adults are reported to reach roughly 90–120 cm total length, which translates to a disc width on the order of 45–60 cm; a tank footprint with at least 90 cm of front-to-back depth and a long open sandy area is widely advised.

A deep, soft, fine-sand substrate is important: it allows the species to bury itself for camouflage — a behavior typical of the group — and prevents abrasion injuries to the underside of the disc. Coarser or sharp substrates have been associated with abdominal scratching and subsequent infection. The tail bears a venomous defensive spine, so handling and netting should be undertaken with care.

Like other sharks and rays (collectively called elasmobranchs), the Fiddler Stingray is highly sensitive to copper-based medications and should never be exposed to them. Maintaining stable water chemistry and avoiding crowding with faster or more aggressive feeders is generally advised.
breedingNotes: Ovoviviparous; females produce golden egg capsules containing up to three embryos, giving birth to live young (typically 2–3 pups per cycle) at approximately 25 cm total length. Successful captive breeding has not been widely reported and is not considered practical for private aquariums.

additional.notes: Third source for tank size (400 US gal / ~1514 L), temperature (72–78°F / ~22–26°C), pH (8.1–8.4), specific gravity (1.020–1.025), care level (Expert), and behavior (uses sandy substrate for camouflage, can jump from open-top tanks).

fish.breedingNotes: Ovoviviparous; not reliably bred in private aquariums. A small number of public aquariums have reported births.
